binaries.title: http.sys - No security-relevant change (WIL feature-staging cache bookkeeping bit 0x40000 in wil_details_FeatureStateCache_ReevaluateCachedFeatureEnabledState)

changed_functions.name: wil_details_FeatureStateCache_ReevaluateCachedFeatureEnabledState (sub_1400BF5E8)
key_changes: PATCHED version adds 'bts eax, 0x12' (set bit 0x40000) at 0x1400bf654, unconditionally ORing the recomputed WIL feature-state cache word with 0x40000 before the interlocked compare-exchange store. This bit is not set in the unpatched version.
Unpatched: the compare-exchange loop publishes the recomputed cache value without bit 0x40000 (cmpxchg at 0x1400bf68e writes ebx).
Patched: the loop forces bit 0x40000 on (cmpxchg at 0x1400bf69e writes esi, derived from the bts-ed value).
This is the ONLY behavioral change; remaining differences are register reallocation (extra push, rbx->rdi, r15->r12) from the added instruction.
changed_functions.security_notes: sub_1400BF5E8 is wil_details_FeatureStateCache_ReevaluateCachedFeatureEnabledState, a Windows Implementation Library (WIL) feature-staging helper that http.sys links in. It recomputes and republishes the cached 'feature enabled state' for a staged feature using an _InterlockedCompareExchange retry loop against a WIL per-feature cache word. It reads the current state via wil_details_GetCurrentFeatureEnabledState (sub_1400BF74C) and optionally subscribes to feature-configuration changes. The patched build forces bit 0x40000 (bit 18) into every published cache word; the unpatched build omits it. This is a WIL cache-state bookkeeping bit introduced by a newer WIL library revision. It is private to the WIL feature-staging subsystem; it is not a timer state, a connection-object field, or a lifetime/ownership sentinel, and no HTTP request, timer, or connection-teardown path consumes it. There is no timer state machine, no DPC, no connection lifecycle, and no use-after-free on this path. Not security-relevant.

changed_functions.name: wil_details_RegisterFeatureUsageProvider (sub_1401C368C)
key_changes: WIL feature-usage provider registration: calls RtlRegisterFeatureUsageProvider with updated provider GUID pointer (0x1401a17f8 -> 0x1401a1818) and config data pointer (0x1401a1de0 -> 0x1401a1dc8).
Added initialization of two new stack fields passed as additional parameters to the registration call.

WIL usage-reporting callback table entry moved from wil_details_RecordFeatureUsageReporting (sub_1400BFB00) to the same function at its shifted address (sub_1400BFB10); rebuild address shift only, target function identical in both builds.
changed_functions.security_notes: Standard WIL feature-usage provider registration churn from a rebuild with updated feature GUIDs. Not security-relevant.

security_findings.entry_point: Internal WIL feature-flag evaluation (invoked when http.sys queries a staged feature whose cached state needs recomputation). Not reachable with attacker-controlled HTTP data.
call_chain: http.sys code evaluates a WIL staged feature flag
publishes recomputed value via _InterlockedCompareExchange into the WIL per-feature cache location
security_findings.vulnerability_class: None. Library feature-staging bookkeeping change (added cache-state bit).
security_findings.description: sub_1400BF5E8 is wil_details_FeatureStateCache_ReevaluateCachedFeatureEnabledState, a WIL feature-staging helper. It recomputes the packed 32-bit WIL feature-enabled-state cache word and republishes it with an interlocked compare-exchange loop. The unpatched build publishes the recomputed value without bit 0x40000; the patched build forces bit 0x40000 on via 'bts eax, 0x12' before the store. The cache word lives entirely inside WIL's feature-state cache; the bits it manipulates (0x2, 0x4, 0x400, masks 0x9C1 / 0xFFFFF63E) are WIL feature-enabled-state packing fields, not timer flags. Bit 0x40000 is a WIL cache-state bookkeeping bit added by a newer WIL revision. No connection object, timer, or DPC is involved. The addresses that a use-after-free narrative would require resolve to unrelated helpers: sub_1400BE740 = UxStartEnvironmentModule (settings/environment startup, not a timer DPC); sub_1400BF2E4 = wil_details_FeatureReporting_RecordUsageInCache; sub_1400BF868 = wil_details_IsEnabledFallback; sub_1400BD624 = UxReadBooleanSetting; sub_1400BD680 = UxReadBoundedULongSetting; 0x1401620E0 = _guard_dispatch_icall (CFG dispatch). There is no use-after-free and no security-relevant change.
security_findings.trigger_conditions: Not remotely triggerable as a vulnerability. The function runs on internal WIL feature-flag evaluation and operates only on the WIL feature-state cache word. To simply observe the changed bit in a debugger: break at the interlocked store (0x1400bf68e unpatched / 0x1400bf69e patched) and read the published value - unpatched never has bit 0x40000, patched always does. The pointer register (r15 unpatched / r12 patched) is the WIL per-feature cache location, not a connection or timer object.
security_findings.primitive: None. Toggling a WIL feature-cache bookkeeping bit cannot free, dangle, or corrupt any kernel object. No exploit primitive exists.
security_findings.assembly:
=== UNPATCHED wil_details_FeatureStateCache_ReevaluateCachedFeatureEnabledState @ 0x1400BF5E8 (loop region) ===
0x1400bf646: mov     ecx, ebx
0x1400bf648: mov     dword [rsp+0x58], ebx
0x1400bf64c: test    esi, esi
0x1400bf64e: je      0x1400bf671
0x1400bf650: mov     dword [rsp+0x58], ebx
0x1400bf654: test    cl, 0x2
0x1400bf657: jne     0x1400bf671
0x1400bf659: mov     ebx, ecx
0x1400bf65b: mov     eax, edi
0x1400bf65d: and     ebx, 0xfffff63e
0x1400bf663: and     eax, 0x9c1
0x1400bf668: or      ebx, eax
0x1400bf66a: or      ebx, 0x2
0x1400bf66d: mov     dword [rsp+0x58], ebx
0x1400bf671: test    cl, 0x4
0x1400bf674: jne     0x1400bf68c
0x1400bf676: btr     ebx, 0xa
0x1400bf67a: mov     eax, edi
0x1400bf67c: and     eax, 0x400
0x1400bf681: or      eax, ebx
0x1400bf683: or      eax, 0x4
0x1400bf686: mov     ebx, eax
0x1400bf688: mov     dword [rsp+0x58], eax
0x1400bf68c: mov     eax, ecx
0x1400bf68e: lock cmpxchg dword [r15], ebx   ; publishes cache word WITHOUT bit 0x40000
0x1400bf693: je      0x1400bf69b

=== PATCHED wil_details_FeatureStateCache_ReevaluateCachedFeatureEnabledState @ 0x1400BF5E8 (loop region) ===
0x1400bf650: mov     eax, edi
0x1400bf652: mov     ecx, edi
0x1400bf654: bts     eax, 0x12          ; ADDED: set bit 0x40000
0x1400bf658: mov     dword [rsp+0x58], eax
0x1400bf65c: mov     esi, eax
0x1400bf65e: test    ebp, ebp
0x1400bf660: je      0x1400bf67d
0x1400bf662: test    cl, 0x2
0x1400bf665: jne     0x1400bf67d
0x1400bf667: and     esi, 0xfffff63e
0x1400bf66d: mov     eax, ebx
0x1400bf66f: and     eax, 0x9c1
0x1400bf674: or      esi, eax
0x1400bf676: or      esi, 0x2
0x1400bf679: mov     dword [rsp+0x58], esi
0x1400bf67d: test    dil, 0x4
0x1400bf681: jne     0x1400bf69c
0x1400bf683: mov     eax, esi
0x1400bf685: mov     ecx, ebx
0x1400bf687: and     ecx, 0x400
0x1400bf68d: btr     eax, 0xa
0x1400bf691: mov     esi, ecx
0x1400bf693: or      esi, eax
0x1400bf695: or      esi, 0x4
0x1400bf698: mov     dword [rsp+0x58], esi
0x1400bf69c: mov     eax, edi
0x1400bf69e: lock cmpxchg dword [r12], esi   ; publishes cache word WITH bit 0x40000
0x1400bf6a4: je      0x1400bf6ac

breakpoints.why: The interlocked compare-exchange that publishes the WIL feature-state cache word. Unpatched writes ebx (no bit 0x40000); patched writes esi (bit 0x40000 forced on). The pointer register (r15 unpatched / r12 patched) is the WIL per-feature cache location, not a connection or timer object.

watch: [r15]/[r12] (dword) = WIL feature-state cache word at the interlocked store; bit 18 (0x40000) is clear in unpatched, set in patched
key_offsets: 0x1400bf68e (unpatched) / 0x1400bf69e (patched): interlocked compare-exchange publishing the WIL feature-state cache word
0x1400bf654 (patched): 'bts eax, 0x12' sets bit 0x40000; in unpatched the equivalent position has no such instruction
0x1401620E0: _guard_dispatch_icall (CFG-guarded indirect call to the WIL subscription callbacks)
debugging_aids.trigger_setup: Not a vulnerability trigger. To observe the changed bit: break at the interlocked store (0x1400bf68e unpatched / 0x1400bf69e patched) and read the published dword; unpatched never has bit 0x40000, patched always does.
debugging_aids.expected_observation: At the interlocked store: the published WIL feature-state cache word lacks bit 0x40000 in the unpatched build and has it in the patched build. No object is freed, and no crash results from this difference.
debugging_aids.notes: The cache word is a packed WIL feature-enabled-state bitfield (masks 0x9C1, 0xFFFFF63E, plus bits 0x2, 0x4, 0x400). Bit 0x40000 is a WIL cache-state bookkeeping bit added by a newer WIL library revision. sub_1400BF5E8 = wil_details_FeatureStateCache_ReevaluateCachedFeatureEnabledState, sub_1400BF74C = wil_details_GetCurrentFeatureEnabledState, sub_1400BF2E4 = wil_details_FeatureReporting_RecordUsageInCache, sub_1400BF868 = wil_details_IsEnabledFallback, sub_1400BE740 = UxStartEnvironmentModule, sub_1400BD624 = UxReadBooleanSetting, sub_1400BD680 = UxReadBoundedULongSetting, 0x1401620E0 = _guard_dispatch_icall. None of these is a timer DPC or connection-lifecycle routine.

summary: The two changed functions are both WIL (Windows Implementation Library) feature-staging helpers that http.sys links in, not HTTP connection or timer code. wil_details_FeatureStateCache_ReevaluateCachedFeatureEnabledState (sub_1400BF5E8) recomputes and republishes the WIL cached feature-enabled state via an interlocked compare-exchange loop; the patched build forces bit 0x40000 into every published cache word ('bts eax, 0x12' at 0x1400bf654), while the unpatched build omits it. This bit is private to WIL's feature-state cache bookkeeping and is not consumed as a timer/connection lifetime sentinel. The second function, wil_details_RegisterFeatureUsageProvider (sub_1401C368C), has updated feature-usage provider GUID/config pointers. Both are WIL feature-staging library churn from a version bump. There is no timer state machine, no DPC, no connection lifecycle, and no use-after-free. No security-relevant change.
